10 Rookie Draft Mistakes Your Buffoon Le:
1. Trade up into the top six prospects wherever possible in dynasty rookie drafts.

2. Accumulate multiple picks in the 2011-312 range where consensus is weak and positional value fluctuates.

3. Trade out of the current rookie draft entirely for future class capital when league mates undervalue upcoming classes like 2027.

4. Never draft for positional need; instead acquire position depth through trades or take best available players.

5. Do your due diligence on likely landing spots before using trades to move picks around consensus.

6. Avoid reaching on prospects solely based on NFL draft capital; evaluate prospect tier within each round, not just the round itself.

7. Don't take what the draft class gives you at a position; trade into better positions or out of the class if needed.

8. Use tie-breaking tools like ADP landing spot charts to trade down safely for players you'll still likely acquire later.

9. Factor offensive scheme fit and coaching alignment into prospect evaluation, not just landing spot opportunity.

10. Make your own evaluations and mistakes; don't blindly follow expert consensus if you have conviction on a different tier.
2026 Rookie QBs to Target & Avoid.txt:
1. Evaluate quarterback prospects by comparing their profile strength to historical comps rather than draft capital alone, as the prospect model is 33.6% more predictive than draft capital alone.

2. In superflex leagues, prioritize veteran quarterbacks or late-round QB prospects with high upside over early-round QB2 prospects with poor historical comps.

3. Target day three quarterbacks with "smash" labels and significant upside (Cole Payton, Drew Aller) over day one QB prospects with limited college experience or weak historical comparisons.

4. Avoid early-round quarterback prospects with fewer than 20 career starts whose historical comparisons include multiple first-contract failures (exception: proven elite tools like mobility or arm strength).

5. Prioritize quarterback prospects with multiple years of starting experience and larger college samples over prospects with limited starting experience, regardless of draft capital.

6. When evaluating QB landing spots, weigh the presence of a quarterback-developer coach more heavily than just weapons availability.

7. Target backup quarterback situations where the starter has injury risk, age concerns, or contract uncertainty for potential breakout value in years 1-2.

8. Trade down from early-round picks to acquire proven veteran QBs or multiple late-round QB prospects rather than selecting a single QB2-upside prospect in the first two rounds.

9. Use the prospect model's traits evaluation (mobility grade) to identify mobile/Konami quarterbacks as preferred over pocket passers for dynasty upside.

10. Accept that post-year-two performance is the true indicator of quarterback hit/miss status, so patience through year one is essential before judging dynasty value.

Default_medium_10 Rookie Draft Mistakes:
# Dynasty Rookie Draft Strategy Rules

1. Trade up into the top six prospects whenever possible to secure tier-one talent.

2. Accumulate volume of picks in the 2011-312 range where consensus is weakest and value variance is highest.

3. Trade out of the current draft class entirely into 2027 or 2028 capital if league mates undervalue future classes.

4. Never draft for positional need, especially when rebuilding; instead take best available and trade current assets to address gaps.

5. Take what the draft class gives you by position rather than forcing selections in weak positions; trade for weakness if competing.

6. Conduct due diligence on league-mate roster needs and target specific teams willing to move up rather than making generic trade offers.

7. Verify landing spot probability using ADP data before trading down to secure a player; avoid trading down too far on targets.

8. Use landing spot as a tiebreaker within tiers, not as a reason to draft a lesser prospect multiple rounds early.

9. Prioritize scheme and offensive fit over landing spot for wide receivers, as talent typically overcomes circumstances.

10. Factor in organizational fit, coaching system compatibility, and positional role alignment when evaluating prospects.

11. Do not outsmart consensus on top-tier players; instead trade down when your rankings align with late-round ADP expectations.

12. Make your own mistakes and maintain conviction in contrarian picks rather than blindly following expert rankings.

13. Execute multiple trades using incremental value gains rather than attempting home runs, as consistent deal-making compounds advantages.

# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. Prioritize landing spot over prospect talent when the gap in NFL draft capital is significant (e.g., second round vs. third round RB, top-20 pick vs. mid-round pick).

2. Target running backs with second-round NFL draft capital over third-round backs, as second-round backs have higher hit rates for immediate starting opportunities.

3. Fade wide receivers going to teams with established elite receiving options regardless of prospect grade, unless the current starter is aging out.

4. Prioritize tight ends with top-50 NFL draft capital and clear long-term roles over lower-capital tight ends with better short-term landing spots.

5. Take calculated risks on higher-graded prospects in suboptimal landing spots over lower-graded prospects with perfect situations.

6. Target quarterbacks only if they have legitimate tools to become top-tier NFL starters, not simply because they fill a team need.

7. Avoid prospects with off-field concerns entirely, especially "boom-or-bust" profile types who lack other mitigating factors.

8. Use NFL free agency timelines to identify opportunity windows (e.g., if a starter is UFA after current season, target their positional replacement).

9. Prefer receivers with high-level separation and coverage-beating skills over pure production metrics, as tape evaluation reveals true translatable traits.

10. Target complementary skill-set receivers to existing team weapons rather than redundant profiles in crowded receiving corps.

11. Invest in receiving tight ends paired with deep-ball capable quarterbacks in their landing spots.

12. Prioritize film study over measurable testing results when evaluating small-school or non-traditional conference prospects.

# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. Prioritize draft capital and landing spot alignment; when a prospect has both, their value increases significantly compared to one factor alone.

2. Trade up for premium landing spots (top-10 capital with elite offenses) when available, as they dramatically improve rookie-year production ceilings.

3. In weak prospect classes, stack third and fourth-round rookie picks rather than rely on a single late second-round pick.

4. Sell late second-round picks for established productive veterans if the rookie class lacks depth at your position of need.

5. When prospect talent is close, defer to better landing spots and quarterback situations over marginal talent differences.

6. Target tight ends earlier than usual in weak receiver/running back draft classes, as positional scarcity increases their relative value.

7. Don't waste premium picks on prospects with poor landing spots; trade down to accumulate additional picks instead.

8. Prioritize quarterback situation stability over draft capital when evaluating skill position prospects, especially for year-one production.

9. In dynasty drafts with weak classes, accept lower-floor prospects with high-ceiling upside in ambiguous backfields or receiver rooms.

10. Trade veteran players for multiple rookie picks (2-for-1) when the talent drop-off between rounds 2-4 is minimal.

11. Handcuff aging veterans with young prospects to the same team for potential year-two breakout scenarios.

12. Ignore prospect consensus rankings when landing spots create significant advantage; trust your evaluation and lean into value discrepancies.

# Dynasty Startup Strategy Rules

1. Evaluate your league market and identify the tendencies of your specific league mates before draft day.

2. Know your league settings (roster construction, scoring format, league size) and adjust your strategy accordingly.

3. Conduct mock drafts to understand positional value and identify where you like picking in your draft order.

4. Stay flexible in the first 3-4 rounds by avoiding purely competitive or rebuilding assets that pigeonhole your team direction.

5. Use rookie picks and future assets as draft picks to delay committing to a specific team build.

6. Identify which positions will fall in your specific league and exploit those value pockets.

7. Once you commit to a competitive or rebuilding direction, stick with it consistently for the remainder of the draft.

8. Don't get stuck in the middle—be aggressively competitive or aggressively rebuild rather than wavering between both approaches.

9. Trade proactively during the startup by contacting other managers before your picks to facilitate value exchanges.

10. Take the best available player on the board rather than forcing position scarcity, as you can trade assets immediately after the draft.

11. Understand opportunity bundles—how different position combinations in early rounds affect your overall team construction.

12. Use league-specific trade calculators to evaluate all trade offers during the startup.

# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. In Superflex leagues, secure two anchor quarterbacks in the early-to-middle rounds rather than waiting for QB depth to fall, as the position tier drops significantly after QB10-12.

2. Trade mid-round rookie picks (106-108 range) from weak landing spot scenarios for future second-round picks to increase draft capital for in-season trades at the deadline.

4. Sell high on second-round NFL draft pick quarterbacks after strong rookie seasons before regression occurs, rather than holding for long-term value.

# Fantasy Football Dynasty Strategy Rules

1. In startup drafts, prioritize "anchor players" — those likely to be foundational assets at their position for years — over upside lottery tickets in early rounds.

2. Avoid using early rookie picks on players who have already shown significant red flags in college (injury history, lack of workload despite talent).

3. Don't overvalue late-first-round rookie picks; focus on which established assets you rank them ahead of rather than absolute positioning.

4. Build quarterback flexibility by not going heavy on QB early, as the QB10-24 tier has flattened enough to find value at skill positions instead.

5. Trade veteran assets before the NFL Draft to capitalize on playoff/end-of-season momentum before offseason narratives change perception.

6. Use your positional roster construction to guide pick usage — trade down if you have positional depth rather than reaching for positional scarcity.

7. When trading picks for established players, factor in contract years remaining and age; a young player on a rookie deal with uncertain opportunity is different from a veteran.

8. Don't chase single-year efficiency anomalies as evidence a player is overvalued; evaluate whether circumstances (scheme change, injury return) genuinely explain performance.

9. Separate prospect ability from NFL opportunity — a talented player losing snaps isn't a reflection on talent, but dynasty value depends on opportunity.

10. Patrol the trade market in the months between leagues; established players' values shift based on coaching changes and landing spots before the wider market recognizes it.

# Fantasy Football Dynasty Strategy Rules from 2026 RB Rankings Transcript

1. In weak positional classes, identify and target the few elite prospects at the top regardless of overall class quality, as they provide disproportionate advantage.

2. Prioritize watching film breakdowns over prospect testing times, as on-field performance metrics (yards after contact, elusiveness rating) better predict NFL success than combine athleticism.

3. Target running backs with elite contact balance and vision even if they lack top-end speed, as these traits correlate with fantasy relevance better than athletic testing numbers.

4. Avoid running backs lacking refinement in pad level, pass protection, and reading defenses despite high athletic traits, as these indicate they won't earn three-down workloads.

5. Discount running backs from committees or limited roles in college until proven otherwise, as production volume matters more than prospect pedigree when projecting NFL opportunity.

6. Target undervalued running backs with 75+ FSE grades projected after round 2 in rookie drafts, as market inefficiency creates draft capital mismatch with actual talent.

7. Exploit sell-high windows immediately after weak RB classes land in NFL, as early season success from average prospects creates inflated trade value.

8. Deprioritize receiving-only running backs without demonstrated between-the-tackle ability, as NFL coaches won't grant three-down workloads to one-dimensional profiles.

9. Target running backs with multi-year college starting experience over one-year wonders, as consistency and durability better predict sustainable NFL roles.

10. Avoid running backs under 6'0" or under 205 lbs without elite contact balance, as they face structural obstacles to securing full workloads regardless of elusiveness.

11. Examine injury history context in college, as long-term injuries limiting production volume distort talent evaluation more than they indicate NFL ceiling.

12. Target running backs from underutilized backfield situations if film shows NFL-caliber instincts and decision-making, as offensive context often masks talent better than it reveals it.

1. Prioritize wide receivers with strong measurables (40 time, vertical, broad jump) over draft pedigree when evaluating small school prospects, as elite athleticism significantly increases NFL draft capital projection.

2. Target wide receivers whose combine performance confirms pre-combine projections rather than those who surprise significantly, as expected performance indicates reliable film evaluation.

3. Discount narrow-sized receivers (under 5'10") in dynasty drafts regardless of athleticism, as the fantasy-relevant track record for sub-5'10" wide receivers is extremely limited.

4. Weight production profile and yards-per-route-run metrics more heavily than isolated athletic testing when evaluating mid-to-late round prospects, as athleticism alone does not guarantee NFL success.

5. Favor wide receivers with elite separation and agility traits over those relying primarily on size and speed, as separation ability more directly correlates to fantasy production.

6. Monitor pro day testing for top-ranked prospects who did not compete at the NFL Combine, as missing combine data creates evaluation uncertainty until official measurements are confirmed.

7. Apply skepticism to significant upticks in draft capital projections for small school wide receivers following strong combine testing, waiting for production-based validation before investing dynasty assets.
Default_medium_2026 Rookie WRs to Target:
# Fantasy Football Strategy Rules - Dynasty League Focus

1. Target prospects with superior film grades and analytics over those selected purely based on draft capital, especially when draft capital is applied to unproven or inconsistent performers.

2. Prioritize wide receivers with elite contested catch rates and zone-beating abilities, as these traits correlate strongly with sustained NFL production.

3. Avoid chasing draft capital alone; instead, use prospect grades and landing spot analysis to identify undervalued players who fell due to draft scarcity rather than talent deficiency.

4. Buy the dip on talented prospects in suboptimal landing spots when the situation can realistically improve (quarterback turnover, offensive changes, depth chart openings in year 2-3).

5. Don't stack multiple receivers from the same team in the same rookie draft; instead, distribute shots across prospects to hedge cannibalization risk on overlapping depth charts.

6. Differentiate between year-one fantasy production expectations and long-term dynasty value; patience picks can provide superior returns if landing spots improve.

7. Focus on production history and consistency over college program or recent tape; multi-year producers with steady volume typically outperform boom-or-bust prospects.

8. Identify offenses that will improve due to complementary weapons added in the same draft class, as rising tide scenarios benefit all skill position players in that ecosystem.

9. Target second and third-round receivers in rebuilding situations where they receive immediate opportunity and volume over first-round reaches destined for committees.

10. Use UFA and taxi squad assignments to identify prospect bargains who fell due to off-field concerns but possess elite measurables and production metrics.
Default_medium_2027 Rookie RBs to Target:
1. Target young running backs (age 20-21) in dynasty drafts as they have multiple college seasons remaining to improve their profiles.

2. Prioritize running backs with receiving upside in dynasty evaluations, as pass-catching ability significantly increases NFL draft capital and professional value.

3. Trade back aggressively for 2027 first-round picks during rebuilds, as the running back class is projected to be particularly strong at that draft level.

4. Downgrade senior running backs in dynasty rankings compared to junior counterparts with similar current production, due to limited remaining development time.

5. Value underclassmen running backs who lead their teams in carries despite being young, as their production trajectory suggests elite potential.

6. Monitor transfer portal moves for running backs, as scheme changes and backfield competition significantly impact their draft capital projections.

7. Seek running backs with 4.5+ yards after contact per attempt as a baseline indicator of NFL-caliber talent in college production.

8. Prioritize efficiency metrics (yards per carry, yards after contact) over raw volume stats when evaluating younger running back prospects.

9. Avoid overvaluing small running backs (under 200 lbs) solely based on college receiving production, as NFL market value often differs significantly from college role.

10. Target running backs from Power Four schools over Group of Five transfers, as NFL scouts weight conference level heavily in draft projections.

2. Target prospects with elite athletic profiles and efficiency metrics (3+ yards per route run) as indicators of generational talent, regardless of current age or college production volume.

Default_medium_35 Things Every Fantasy O:
1. Don't overweight combine testing results—film evaluation should remain your primary assessment tool.

2. Evaluate size/weight measurements against listed specs, as discrepancies often indicate player priorities that conflict with NFL needs.

3. For undersized/overweight prospects, prioritize 40-time and agility scores as indicators of whether athleticism compensates for frame concerns.

6. For receiving-focused running backs, prioritize pass-catching drill performance over traditional back metrics.

7. Recognize that exceptional combine performances can artificially inflate draft capital for overlooked prospects, creating potential value discrepancies.

11. For trait-dependent prospects (especially undersized players), strong athletic testing can justify higher dynasty investment than film alone would suggest.

13. Prioritize athleticism testing for prospects with incomplete receiving trees to assess developmental ceiling at the NFL level.
Default_medium_Day 1 of NFL Free Agency:
# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. In shallow rosters (25-man, 1QB, start-7), prioritize stud top-tier players as they represent the highest concentration of value.

2. Actively trade to address critical positional weaknesses rather than hoping to fill them through late-round picks in the draft.

3. When rebuilding toward a specific future year, aggressively sell veterans at peak value windows instead of holding them through decline.

4. Target free agent signings with new teams as buy-low opportunities, as landing spot uncertainty depresses player values before confirmation.

5. Trade multiple future picks (two-three later first-rounders) for elite established players if it positions you to compete within 1-2 seasons.

6. Use your draft capital flexibility to both trade up for premium prospects and trade down to accumulate additional year assets rather than strictly drafting by rank.

7. Avoid making trades to "keep league members happy"—focus on maximizing your own roster construction and let other teams manage themselves.

8. When holding injury-prone or aging players in a contention window, identify and execute sell-high trades before durability concerns materialize.

9. Target pass-catchers at new landing spots with high-volume quarterbacks over players staying with inconsistent offenses.

10. In contracts providing 3+ years of control, prioritize younger players over veterans even with comparable current production due to positional lifespan value.
Default_medium_Dynasty Prayer Circle to:

2. Prioritize prospect grades and on-field efficiency metrics over draft capital when evaluating dynasty assets.

8. In best ball leagues, target receivers who are guaranteed playing time even in ambiguous offensive roles.

9. Use draft capital as a tiebreaker between similarly graded prospects, not as your primary evaluation tool.

10. Trade aging elite players for future draft picks when you can secure multiple early selections from a strong projected roster.

Default_medium_Dynasty Trade Targets by:
# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. Trade for 2026 third-round picks and 2027 second-round picks as deal add-ons when rebuilding, as they offer better late-round value than the weak 2025 class.

2. Target veteran running backs entering their prime years (age 24-27) over rookie picks when contending, as proven production beats unproven draft capital.

3. Buy running backs with clear backfield roles and offensive line stability rather than committee backs competing for touches.

4. Acquire wide receivers in year two or three of their career if they showed efficiency metrics or flash performances despite low volume.

5. Trade for quarterbacks entering offenses with newly added elite weapons or coaching changes rather than waiting for draft capital.

6. Sell players after career-best seasons if regression indicators exist (e.g., touchdown luck, injury history, age).

7. Buy low on talented receivers who underperformed due to quarterback or receiver corps injuries rather than skill decline.

8. Avoid paying premium capital for tight ends in loaded tight end classes; instead trade for proven TE1 finishers.

9. Trade for players whose market value doesn't reflect recent production metrics (fantasy points per game, efficiency stats, snap share trends).

10. Prioritize age-adjusted talent over current role when buying, as young talented players can ascend into bigger roles.

11. Fade players entering committees with quality pass-catchers at their position rather than expecting increased opportunity share.

12. Buy running backs with new coaches who previously developed them or similar archetypes at prior stops.
Default_medium_Expected Hit Rates of Eac:

8. Evaluate rookie pick trade values contextually by comparing available talent within each specific draft class, not using static year-to-year valuations.

9. Lean toward high-volume positions (wide receiver depth in 2026) over positional scarcity in weak draft classes to maximize hit rate probability.

Default_medium_It's Joever For These Dyn:
1. Sell low on highly drafted prospects who underperformed in their rookie season, especially when they had opportunity to produce with a good quarterback.

2. Avoid holding third-round or later running back picks unless they clearly win the starting job, as their hit rate is significantly lower than second-round picks.

3. Trade away aging former stars once they sign to new teams, as the hype around their arrival creates a brief sell window before production disappointment.

4. Sell running backs with durability red flags who miss opportunities to take over when the starter gets injured, as they've likely lost the team's trust.

5. Convert underperforming early-year players into third and fourth-round rookie picks, as deeper rounds of recent drafts offer strong depth at multiple positions.

6. Dump backup running backs stuck behind proven starters who the team has invested in, as they will only see work if injury strikes.

7. Trade away young quarterbacks whose teams have signaled they're moving in another direction, as backup status significantly limits future value.

8. Use league roster spot math to identify cut candidates—calculate total rostered players divided by league size and cut anyone ranked below that threshold.

9. Sell athletic quarterbacks with injury concerns after they lose starting opportunities, as their remaining upside is limited and declining.

10. Cut tight ends at teams that are clearly going to draft a replacement, even if they have upside potential.

11. Trade away second-year players who were lower-rated prospects and failed to establish themselves, as age is working against their development timeline.

12. Avoid sunk cost fallacy—trade or cut players based on current value and future outlook, not on draft capital spent.

Default_medium_Make Better Dynasty Fanta:
# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. Evaluate your leaguemate's team and needs before sending a trade offer, not just your own roster construction.

2. Send fair initial offers that are 10-20% in your favor rather than fleece deals to encourage counter-offers and trade discussions.

3. Follow up every trade offer with a direct message explaining your rationale and thought process behind the deal.

4. Identify your team's biggest weakness and target league managers who have depth at that position.

5. Target buy-low windows on players with recent negative publicity or injury concerns to maximize trade value.

6. Consolidate multiple depth pieces into one higher-end asset when you have redundancy at a position.

7. Use your positional strengths to trade away depth and upgrade weaknesses elsewhere.

8. Customize trade offers to match a leaguemate's situation—picks for rebuilders, production for contenders.

9. Be honest and transparent in DM communications about your evaluation of their team's competitive window.

10. Send multiple offer variations to the same manager showing different paths (rebuild vs. compete scenarios).

11. Follow up with "no worries, let me know if things change" messaging after rejected offers to maintain future trade relationships.

12. Actively send 25-30 trade offers across your leagues expecting only 10-15% acceptance rate through volume and persistence.

13. Adjust offer values based on league scoring settings that create positional scarcity (e.g., TE premium, carry bonuses).

14. Target players with recent negative news or injury concerns when they're undervalued by scared managers.

12. Trade away veterans whose teams just drafted premium prospects at their position before draft capital reduces their value.

# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. Trade running backs whose teams show heavy interest in multiple RB prospects in pre-draft visits, as 81% of teams with 3+ RB visits draft a RB in the first four rounds.

2. Sell established running backs before the draft if you can receive early-to-mid second-round capital or better, as rookie RBs significantly disrupt opportunity-dependent backfields.

3. Trade wide receivers coming off career-best seasons before the draft if their team has shown 7+ visits to WR prospects, as 81% of such teams draft a WR in the first three rounds.

4. Avoid selling tight ends whose teams have drafted few tight end prospects in pre-draft visits, as limited due diligence signals the incumbent TE's role is safer.

5. Sell older running backs (age 28+) with high touch counts whose teams possess multiple top-70 picks and have scouted fewer RB prospects, as they risk committee situations with premium draft picks.

6. Trade backup quarterbacks for second-round picks when their team has shown 3+ QB visits, as teams with heavy QB due diligence frequently draft a QB despite starter performance.

7. Prioritize selling players whose teams have shown heavy positional interest in the draft over players whose teams show minimal interest in their position group.

8. Package mid-tier asset players with undervalued veterans to trade up for higher-ceiling players before draft capital and landing spots are revealed.

9. Sell tight ends with high touchdown totals who lack high route participation if their team has visited multiple TE prospects, as snap-based regression is likely with draft additions.

10. Avoid selling players to league mates until after the draft is complete if you have uncertainty about incoming competition, as landing spots provide clarity on true disruption risk.
Default_medium_Sell Your 2026 Rookie Pic:
1. Sell rookie picks now before the combine, as their value increases closer to the draft regardless of class quality.

2. Don't trade early first-round picks for aging veteran players; prioritize assets with 2-3 year windows instead.

3. If you don't need a positional upgrade, trade your pick for a prospect rather than selling it for established players.

4. Trade early picks (101-104) for young quarterback assets when rebuilding, not aging veteran QBs.

5. Target running backs with upcoming offensive coordinator/quarterback upgrades when trading picks in the 102-109 range.

6. Trade end-of-first-round picks (110-112) now rather than hold them, as this range typically underperforms post-NFL draft.

7. Prioritize dynasty assets with proven NFL production over prospects with uncertain landing spots at the same pick value.

8. Buy low on talented players dealing with temporary setbacks (injury, bad situation, scheme mismatch) over mid-tier prospects.

9. Don't reach for prospects in heavy premium positions; trade picks for proven players at those positions instead.

10. Use add-ons (future picks) to trade up from mid-round picks rather than trading them straight for younger prospects.
Default_medium_The Only Dynasty SF Rooki:
# Dynasty Rookie Draft Strategy Rules

1. Draft elite prospects over draft capital when the prospect grade doesn't match the pick value.

2. Target prospects with clear offensive roles and ascending quarterbacks over ambiguous depth charts.

3. Trade down from mid-first round picks (12-18) to accumulate additional draft selections in the same round.

4. Prioritize prospects from power conference schools with multiple years of production over one-year wonders.

5. Exploit landing spot inefficiencies by taking talented prospects to bad situations before the market corrects.

6. Bet on coaches with demonstrated track records of starting young skill players regardless of draft capital.

7. Accept that tight ends drafted in the second round have similar long-term upside to early-round receivers in weak classes.

8. Fade quarterbacks in ambiguous depth chart situations unless they have legitimate injury-related path to starts.

9. Avoid receivers who are the third option in new offenses without significant pass volume allocation.

10. Take receiving talent over running back capital in weak draft classes when prospect grades are similar.

11. Stack pass-heavy offenses with multiple receiving prospects from the same team.

12. Discount year-one production projections for talented prospects in bad immediate situations.

13. Prioritize athletic upside swings on day-two receivers over reliable floor guys in this draft class.

14. Avoid sacrificial X receivers on run-heavy teams unless they have elite separation skills.

15. Trust scheme fits and coordinator history over just landing spot pedigree.
Default_medium_The Sleepers Everyone's M:
1. Target undersized running backs with elite elusiveness and receiving traits in the 3rd-4th rounds, as they can provide depth and potential breakout opportunity in their second season.

2. In Superflex leagues, prioritize quarterback prospects with elite trait grades and rushing ability even from lower-level competition, as they can develop into high-value backups or eventual starters.

3. Draft tight ends with premium draft capital and ideal role fit over pure production, as landing spot and offensive system usage matter more than college statistics.

4. Identify running back prospects with strong character and work ethic to pair as handcuffs to aging veterans, as they're poised to inherit significant workload within 2-3 seasons.

5. Target wide receivers from weak offensive situations who display nuanced route-running and competitiveness at the catch point, as they can immediately contribute in better landing spots.

6. In tight end premium formats, prioritize athletes with elite size, catch radius, and red zone potential over production, as these traits translate most predictably to NFL impact.

7. Accumulate multiple picks in the 3rd-4th round range through trades rather than taking single players, increasing your probability of hitting on high-upside lottery tickets.

8. Roster quarterback prospects with 4.4-4.6 combine speed on your taxi squad, as they can develop into valuable bye-week fill-ins or injury replacements within 2-3 years.

9. Prioritize wide receiver prospects landing with QB play from proven passing offenses, as coaching track record with receiver development increases floor value.

10. Stack complementary traits (athleticism + production + landing spot) over any single metric when evaluating 3rd-4th round prospects, as confluence of factors determines breakout probability.
Default_medium_These Dynasty Rookies Are:
1. Do not overvalue prospects based solely on physical size and measurables if they lack the skill development to match (route polish, agility, separation ability).

2. Prioritize route refinement and footwork efficiency over aesthetic movement or highlight-tape performance when evaluating wide receiver prospects.

3. Discount running back prospects who lack pass-protection ability and show poor vision in gap recognition, as this limits their NFL usage and touch ceiling.

4. Apply significant caution to injury-prone prospects (especially ACL tears) in dynasty rookie drafts, as recovery timelines often prevent skill development and limit rookie-year production.

5. Avoid reaching on quarterback prospects in superflex formats based purely on draft capital if film study reveals concerning mechanical or decision-making flaws.

6. Heavily weight one-year starters with durability concerns and limited size at quarterback, as this combination historically produces bust-prone prospects.

7. Cross-reference your own film evaluations against draft capital projections—do not automatically assume first-round NFL draft picks warrant early dynasty rookie picks if tape analysis contradicts the hype.

8. Be skeptical of prospects who appear more talented on highlight reels than in all-22 film, as production volume alone can mask limited actual skill development.

9. Factor anticipated draft capital into dynasty rookie valuations, as teams may reach on draft day and inflate fantasy prices regardless of prospect grade.

10. Prioritize receiving skill versatility and contact balance in running back evaluations over raw receiving output, as limited pass-protection ability restricts snap counts.
Default_medium_Top 12 Dynasty Superflex:
1. Target Jeremiah Love at 1.01 in all dynasty rookie drafts regardless of landing spot, as his profile projects to elite fantasy production.

2. Prioritize receiving versatility in running back prospects, as three-down backs with slot capability have the highest fantasy upside.

3. Evaluate wide receivers based on fantasy production potential rather than NFL prospect ranking, as zone-beating ability and target volume matter more than athleticism alone.

4. Downgrade wide receiver prospects who lack consistent yards-after-catch instincts, even if their route running and athleticism grade well on tape.

5. Use expected points-per-game projections from prospect models to compare fantasy upside across different positions and break positional tiers.

6. Target tight ends capable of playing both inline and slot positions, as alignment versatility creates size-mismatch advantages in modern NFL offenses.

7. Don't discount running back prospects with limited college touches if their film shows elite instincts, contact balance, and breakaway ability.

8. Value developed, mature receivers with strong red zone dominance and zone awareness over raw athletes, as they convert targets to fantasy points more consistently.

9. Rank prospects partially by expected NFL draft capital, as first-round picks to your preferred landing spots deserve premium dynasty grades.

10. Identify fit-dependent prospects and monitor landing spots closely, as scheme utilization can swing a player's fantasy ceiling by multiple tiers.

11. Prefer quarterbacks with decisive decision-making and pocket presence over scrambling ability, as decision-making translates more reliably to fantasy production.

12. Use prospect grades and trait scores together—high trait grades with lower analytics grades indicate coachable weaknesses that may improve quickly at the next level.
Default_medium_Top 25 Dynasty Superflex:
1. Prioritize early declaration status over production pedigree when evaluating wide receivers, as non-early declares have significantly lower NFL success rates.

2. Value tight ends who can operate in multiple personnel packages (11 personnel and move roles) rather than pure move tight ends limited to specific alignments.

3. Take calculated swings on high-ceiling prospects in the second-third round range rather than settling for safe floor picks, as hit rates in this range are only ~30% regardless.

4. Accumulate multiple mid-round picks (2.04, 2.06, 2.08) over top-two picks in weaker classes, as value compression makes late-second round picks nearly equivalent to early-second.

5. Exploit consensus downgrade opportunities on talented players with injury recoveries by ranking them above their expected draft capital.

6. Discount single-year college starters at quarterback unless they showed near-perfect tape, as historical success rate is significantly lower than multi-year starters.

7. Weigh contact balance and receiving ability heavily for running backs, as these skills separate NFL contributors from non-factors regardless of production context.

8. Favor refined route runners with contest catch ability over elite athlete receivers with raw route trees, as route nuance is harder to develop than athleticism.

9. Devalue receivers from scheme-heavy offenses (Tennessee, USC) until they prove skills translate in neutral NFL settings.

10. Prioritize functional athleticism and measurable traits over production when evaluating FCS/Group of Five wide receivers, as competition level obscures true NFL-level ability.
Default_medium_We Rate Your Dynasty Fant:
# Fantasy Football Dynasty Strategy Rules

1. Buy running backs entering their second season if they showed physical talent as rookies but lacked consistency, as they typically improve significantly with additional NFL experience.

2. Trade away veteran running backs before free agency reveals landing spots, as their value can spike substantially if they join high-volume offenses.

3. Avoid trading your early rookie picks immediately in the offseason; hold them until closer to the draft when their value relative to free agent moves becomes clearer.

4. In rebuild situations with multiple contenders ahead of you, aggressively trade older productive assets and consolidate future draft capital rather than attempting win-now trades.

5. Trade down from premium picks if you can acquire multiple first-round talents through negotiation, rather than accepting one-for-one offers.

6. When a young tight end shows promise in his rookie season, prioritize acquiring him over veteran options as tight ends typically improve significantly in year two.

7. Buy second and third-round talents whose college tape shows elite traits but lacked opportunity, as NFL volume in better situations can unlock their upside.

8. Trade tight ends coming off career years at peak value, as the position is volatile and unlikely to sustain elite production.

9. Acquire young defensive skill position players on better teams over older productive players on worse teams, as situation changes often decrease production.

10. Use draft picks strategically to address positional weaknesses rather than reaching for need; depth through later picks is more efficient than early capital on depth.

# Dynasty Fantasy Football Strategy Rules

1. In competitive windows without future draft picks, trade current-year picks for immediate production from established running backs rather than taking chances on prospects with lower hit rates.

2. Sell high on wide receivers after breakout stretches caused by favorable circumstances (injuries to teammates, high passing volume) rather than betting on sustained elite production.

3. When rebuilding, prioritize collecting draft picks and young assets over buying aging veterans, even if it means short-term roster weakness.

4. In non-PPR formats, sell receivers with PPR-dependent value production styles to buyers who value that format more heavily.

5. When caught between competing and rebuilding, identify which direction your asset base naturally supports rather than forcing a middle-ground strategy.

6. Target tight end position value in deeper rounds of rookie drafts, as the position has higher variance and mid-round prospects can return strong value.

7. Trade down from premium draft picks using multiple lower picks when the prospect talent drop-off beyond the top tier is significant.

8. Avoid overexposure to single players across multiple leagues by selling shares when you hold more than two of the same asset.

9. Keep quarterback depth in case your primary option underperforms, but don't waste premium capital on multiple starting-caliber quarterbacks.

10. When your team is competing, move expiring assets (older players, scheme-dependent receivers) to contenders for immediate-window help rather than holding for future value.
